--- SOURCE 1: Agroecological transition ---
URL: https://dicoagroecologie.fr/en/dictionnaire/agroecological-transition/

SUMMARY:
The agroecological transition is a change of the agricultural model in order to implement the principles of agroecology and therefore respond to the sector's crises .

FULL CONTENT:
![Logo dicoAE](/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/logo_dicoae_va2.svg)

# Agroecological transition

Published on 16/06/2017

# Agroecological transition

Published on 16/06/2017

The authors:

[Laurent Hazard](https://dicoagroecologie.fr/auteur/laurent-hazard/)
[Marie-Benoît Magrini](https://dicoagroecologie.fr/auteur/marie-benoit-magrini/)
[Guillaume Martin](https://dicoagroecologie.fr/auteur/guillaume-martin/)

Foreign equivalent(s):

Transition agroécologique (fr) | TAE (fr)

![icone generique](https://dicoagroecologie.fr/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/icn_generique.svg)

The term transition refers to the process by which the principles governing a system are radically altered, resulting in a series of changes of both the values of actors and the techniques they use. Its duration varies from a few years for a production unit, to several decades for a sector of activity or for society as a whole. A transition is a complex process because a system in place is generally locked by the coherence, built over time, between techniques, habits of actors, regulation, etc. Removing these locks requires collective actions such as transition management, and the adoption of a path-making strategy whose ends and means are reconsidered whenever necessary during the transition process.

The agroecological transition is a change of the agricultural model in order to implement the principles of agroecology and therefore respond to the sector’s crises. It is based, in particular, on i) the creation and mobilization of knowledge from agroecology, ii) the involvement of actors (farmers, agricultural advisers …) in the construction of this knowledge to tailor it to different territories, and iii) the territorialisation of agriculture, involving in particular a reconnection of agricultural production with food localism.

This transition results in a co-evolution of technical and social changes in the agricultural sector, depending on those related to food (eating habits, regulation …) or energy. For instance, the adoption of a new pulse in a crop rotation may be hindered by the lack of consumption of this pulse. The agroecological transition is a supported by participatory approaches involving actors, all the more necessary because it faces a multiplicity of operators, both in number (thousands of farms) and professions (from the farmer to the person responsible of mass catering); as well as the complexity of the locks to be lifted, such as for instance the need to diversify agricultural production in a dedicated area.

--- SOURCE 2: Agro-ecological Transition → Term ---
URL: https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/term/agro-ecological-transition/

SUMMARY:
Meaning → The Agro-ecological Transition involves fundamentally redesigning food systems based on ecological principles and social equity.

FULL CONTENT:
![Pollution → Sustainability Directory](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Pollution-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Pollution → Sustainability Directory](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Pollution-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Agro-Ecological Transition](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/agro-ecological-transition-term-image.webp)

# Agro-Ecological Transition

![A stark visual of discarded material, implying pollution from possible toxic waste and mismanagement of recycling. Smoke suggests incineration or chemical emissions, symbolizing environmental degradation. The composition creates unease, highlighting unsustainable practices and the ecological footprint.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/deconstructed-waste-management-material-analysis-revealing-environmental.jpg "Deconstructed Waste Management Material Analysis Revealing Environmental Degradation and Unsustainable Practices through Pollution Examination")

## Fundamentals

The concept known as the Agro-ecological Transition represents a directional shift in agricultural methodology and philosophy. At its core, it describes a move away from conventional, input-intensive farming practices towards systems that prioritize ecological principles and social equity. This initial explanation frames it as a fundamental redesign of how we produce food and manage rural landscapes, emphasizing natural processes over synthetic inputs. Understanding this transformation begins with acknowledging the limitations and externalities of industrial agriculture, recognizing its environmental costs and vulnerabilities.

This designation signifies more than just changing techniques; it involves a comprehensive restructuring of farming systems, food webs, and knowledge exchange networks. Practitioners shift focus towards building healthy soils, conserving water resources, supporting biodiversity, and minimizing pollution. Rather than relying heavily on external inputs like synthetic fertilizers and pesticides, agro-ecological approaches seek to optimize internal farm cycles.

Nutrients are recycled through composting and cover cropping, pests are managed through natural predation and habitat manipulation, and crop diversity enhances resilience against disease and [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  shocks. This transition, from a basic standpoint, involves learning from nature and applying those lessons to cultivate sustenance.

![A study in textures and spheres illustrates interconnectedness within the environment. Sustainable growth is symbolized by moss, demonstrating resilience against pollution. Earth tones emphasize a natural balance, essential for ecosystem health.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/interconnected-spheres-of-natural-textures-highlighting-sustainable-growth.jpg "Interconnected Spheres of Natural Textures Highlighting Sustainable Growth Amidst Environmental Impact")

### Core Components of the Shift

Several key elements define this initial interpretation of moving towards ecologically grounded agriculture. These are the building blocks practitioners begin to assemble.

![A disturbing array of waste illustrates the pervasive impact of pollution on our planet. The discarded items, including plastics, metallic scraps, and decaying organic matter, represent unsustainable consumption and environmental degradation. The scene emphasizes the urgent need for waste management, pollution control, and a transition to a circular economy.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/environmental-degradation-visualized-a-close-look-at-waste-pollution-and.jpg "Environmental Degradation Visualized a Close Look at Waste Pollution and Unsustainable Practices Impacting Our Ecosystem")

### Initial Practice Examples

Beginning the transition often involves adopting specific techniques that align with these principles. These serve as accessible entry points for farmers.

The agro-ecological transition fundamentally re-envisions food production by mimicking natural ecosystems.

Moreover, this early stage of understanding highlights the shift in mindset required. It means viewing the farm as an ecosystem, where interconnections between soil, plants, animals, and humans are paramount. The explication at this level remains relatively simple, focusing on visible practices and immediate environmental benefits.

It contrasts starkly with the industrial model’s focus on maximizing single-commodity yields through standardized, high-input methods. The significance here is in recognizing the need for a different trajectory, one that acknowledges ecological limits and dependencies.

![Floating pieces of waste illustrate the severe environmental impact of current consumption habits. A metal lid, plastic fragments, and other debris showcase the need for sustainable waste management solutions and pollution prevention. The image emphasizes landfill reduction through material recovery and recycling.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/deconstructed-waste-stream-visualizing-pollution-impact-on-landfill-space-and.jpg "Deconstructed Waste Stream Visualizing Pollution Impact on Landfill Space and Sustainable Material Recovery Efforts")

#### Challenges at the Outset

Despite its apparent logic, initiating this transition presents challenges. Farmers often face learning curves for new practices, potential initial yield dips, and lack of supportive infrastructure compared to conventional systems. Understanding the fundamental shift involves recognizing these hurdles alongside the ecological rationale. Support networks, access to knowledge, and tailored financial mechanisms become relevant even at this basic level of comprehension.

Success stories at this stage often involve individual farms or small communities demonstrating the viability of these principles on a limited scale. Their experiences provide tangible examples of how seemingly simple changes, like diversifying crops or building compost, can yield positive environmental outcomes and sometimes unexpected economic benefits through reduced input costs or access to niche markets.

![A deconstructed still life arranged on weathered ground highlights post-consumer waste. Cups, bowls filled with various materials, and discarded items evoke environmental concerns. The composition underscores issues of pollution and resource depletion, emphasizing the urgent need for sustainable practices.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/deconstructed-sustainable-living-an-earthy-composition-of-discarded-items.jpg "Deconstructed Sustainable Living an Earthy Composition of Discarded Items Highlighting Environmental Impact")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the foundational principles, the Agro-ecological Transition takes on a more complex meaning for those with intermediate knowledge. It transforms from a set of practices into a systemic process of change, impacting social, economic, and political dimensions alongside the ecological. This interpretation considers the interplay between farm-level actions and broader food system dynamics.

It’s not merely about what happens on the farm, but how those changes interact with markets, policies, research, and community structures. The designation here evolves to denote a deliberate societal pathway towards resilient and equitable food systems.

This stage of understanding involves analyzing the interconnectedness of agro-ecological practices with supply chains, consumer behavior, and institutional support. For instance, a farmer adopting complex crop rotations might require new market channels for diversified produce, demanding shifts in processing, distribution, and retail. Similarly, reducing synthetic inputs necessitates a deeper understanding of soil biology and pest ecology, calling for different types of research and extension services.

The elucidation at this level probes the feedback loops and leverage points within the food system. Its significance lies in recognizing that individual farm transitions aggregate into broader systemic shifts, requiring coordination and collective action.

![A sustainable culinary creation of spheres within a metal holder demonstrates food sustainability. One textured sphere is brown with green accents, alongside two lighter spheres. This presentation highlights waste reduction, resource efficiency and a commitment to minimize environmental impact.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-food-sphere-design-addressing-food-waste-impact-through-innovative.jpg "Sustainable Food Sphere Design Addressing Food Waste Impact through Innovative Culinary Sustainability Practices")

### Systemic Dimensions

The transition, when viewed from an intermediate perspective, involves grappling with several interconnected system levels. These are not isolated issues but components of a larger transformation.

![A web of plastic waste traps frog spawn, illustrating the devastating impact of pollution on aquatic life and biodiversity loss. The contaminated habitat showcases environmental degradation due to unsustainable waste management. Pollution prevention strategies and circular economy principles are vital to protect ecosystems from industrial effluent and mitigate plastic accumulation, ensuring environmental sustainability and reducing ecosystem disruption.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/pollutions-grip-plastic-waste-entrapment-of-frog-spawn-a-macro-view-of.jpg "Pollution’s Grip Plastic Waste Entrapment of Frog Spawn a Macro View of Environmental Impact and Biodiversity Loss")

### Intermediate Challenges and Strategies

Implementing agro-ecology at this intermediate scale encounters significant hurdles, demanding strategic responses from organizations and experts.

Achieving agro-ecological transition requires aligning farm-level changes with broader market and policy shifts.

Consider organizations working to support this transition. An NGO might focus on establishing farmer field schools to facilitate knowledge exchange on soil health. A cooperative could build infrastructure for aggregating and distributing diversified produce from multiple farms. A food company might invest in developing traceable supply chains that source from farms transitioning to agro-ecological methods.

These examples illustrate the transition as a collaborative endeavor extending beyond the farm gate. The substance of this intermediate phase lies in coordinating efforts across different actors to overcome systemic inertia.

![A stylized dish presentation showcases a sustainable culinary art piece covered in green granules, punctuated by metallic rods, and framed by a naturally derived form. This arrangement evokes thoughts of ecosystem preservation and minimizing environmental footprint. It challenges viewers to consider responsible resource management and sustainable agriculture practices to lessen carbon emissions.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-culinary-art-innovative-food-presentation-symbolizing-minimal.jpg "Sustainable Culinary Art Innovative Food Presentation Symbolizing Minimal Environmental Impact and Resource Depletion")

#### Economic Considerations

The economic viability of agro-ecology at scale is a central concern for experts. Initial investments in perennial crops, infrastructure, or learning new skills can be substantial. However, potential long-term benefits include reduced reliance on costly external inputs, increased resilience to market volatility and climate extremes, and access to higher-value markets for differentiated products.

Analyzing the economics requires a longer time horizon and a broader accounting of costs and benefits, including environmental and social impacts that conventional economics often externalizes. This more sophisticated understanding moves beyond simple yield comparisons to assess profitability within a resilient system.

For organizations, this necessitates developing business models that account for the unique characteristics of agro-ecological production. This could involve structuring procurement contracts to recognize diversified production, [investing Meaning → Investing, through an ESG lens, involves allocating capital to companies that demonstrate strong environmental, social, and governance practices.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  in on-farm or regional infrastructure, or supporting certification schemes that provide clear signals to consumers about ecological practices. The connotation of ‘transition’ here includes adapting economic frameworks to value ecological health and social well-being.

Comparison of Conventional vs. Agro-ecological Approaches (Intermediate View)

| Characteristic | Conventional Agriculture | Agro-ecological Approach |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Reliance** on External Inputs | High (synthetic fertilizers, pesticides) | Low to moderate (focus on internal cycles) |
| **Biodiversity** Management | Low (monoculture prevalence) | High (promoting diverse species) |
| **Knowledge** Source | External (agribusiness, research institutions) | Internal & External (farmer knowledge, participatory research) |
| **System** Focus | Commodity yield maximization | System resilience and health |

![Repurposed pottery vessels adorned with natural fiber rest on lush moss, representing the environmental impact and circular economy principles. The vessels symbolize waste reduction and resource efficiency. These artistic upcycled pieces highlight pollution prevention, ecosystem restoration, and climate action.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-art-of-decay-repurposed-pottery-vessels-embodying-environmental.jpg "Sustainable Art of Decay Repurposed Pottery Vessels Embodying Environmental Impact and Circular Economy Principles")

## Academic

From an academic standpoint, the Agro-ecological Transition is defined as a multi-scalar, complex adaptive systems phenomenon involving the fundamental restructuring of agricultural practices, food systems, and socio-economic relations to align with ecological principles and pursue environmental sustainability, social equity, and economic viability, often contested and shaped by diverse [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics and knowledge paradigms. This academic meaning encapsulates the intellectual complexity and disciplinary breadth required to analyze this transformation rigorously. It is understood not merely as a technical shift but as a profound societal evolution. The explication necessitates drawing from ecology, sociology, economics, political science, and indigenous knowledge systems, recognizing its deep interdisciplinary nature.

The interpretation of this transition within scholarly discourse involves scrutinizing its diverse manifestations across different geographical, cultural, and political contexts. There is no single universal pathway; rather, the specific delineation of agro-ecological practices and their integration into food systems varies significantly based on local ecological conditions, historical legacies, land tenure systems, and prevailing political economies. This academic perspective critically examines how power structures influence the implementation and outcomes of agro-ecological initiatives. The purport is to understand the underlying forces enabling or constraining the transition, moving beyond descriptive accounts to analytical frameworks that explain why and how change occurs (or fails to occur).

![A dark circular vessel presents moss covered soil, symbolizing ecological impact and sustainability. Rough edges imply erosion, potentially restored through sustainable management. Dark and light contrast highlights degraded land undergoing restoration and the need for environmental pollution awareness.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/dark-circular-vessel-holding-mossy-material-displaying-environmental-impact-and.jpg "Dark Circular Vessel Holding Mossy Material Displaying Environmental Impact and Sustainable Growth Potential")

### Analytical Dimensions

Academic analysis delves into the intricate layers and interactions shaping the agro-ecological transition. This requires dissecting the various components and their dynamic relationships.

![Caviar and parsley sit atop a reflective metal plate, highlighting environmental degradation within luxury food. Dark droplets scattered around emphasize potential resource depletion, sustainable sourcing, and pollution concerns. This composition points to the need for waste reduction and the impact of unsustainable agriculture in fine dining.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-gourmet-presentation-caviar-parsley-on-metal-plate-reflecting.jpg "Sustainable Gourmet Presentation Caviar Parsley on Metal Plate Reflecting Environmental Impact and Resource Management")

### Cross-Sectoral Influence → The Pervasive Role of Global Finance

One particularly impactful cross-sectoral influence shaping the agro-ecological transition, often under-scrutinized in simpler interpretations, is the pervasive role of global finance and investment patterns. While public discourse frequently focuses on policy incentives or consumer demand, the vast pools of capital allocated within global financial markets exert a significant, often contradictory, pressure on agricultural systems. Pension funds, asset management firms, private equity funds, and banks direct trillions of dollars into land, agricultural commodities, and food processing infrastructure.

This capital is typically managed with mandates focused on quarterly returns, scale efficiency, and risk [mitigation Meaning → Mitigation focuses on efforts to reduce or prevent the emission of greenhouse gases, tackling the root causes of climate change.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  through standardization and financial instruments. The influence is not merely passive investment; it actively shapes the operational logic of large-scale agriculture.

The conventional financial model inherently favors large-scale, specialized, and standardized operations due to their perceived efficiency and ease of management for distant investors. This preference historically propelled the industrial agricultural model, which aligned well with capital’s requirement for predictable outputs, fungible commodities, and scalable investments. Agro-ecological systems, in contrast, are often characterized by complexity, diversification, smaller scales, reliance on local knowledge, and less predictable biological outcomes compared to tightly controlled industrial processes. They may generate returns differently → perhaps through reduced input costs, ecosystem service provision (which is hard to monetize conventionally), or niche markets → and often require longer time horizons to establish.

Global finance significantly shapes the agro-ecological transition trajectory, often favoring scalable, standardized approaches.

Analyzing this influence requires understanding the financial instruments and investment strategies involved. For instance, investment in farmland as a financial asset class has surged, driven by perceived stability and potential appreciation. These investors often lease land to large operators who continue conventional practices focused on maximizing output per acre, as this metric aligns most directly with lease payments and commodity market performance. Similarly, financing for agricultural inputs (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides) and large-scale infrastructure (processing plants, logistics) remains overwhelmingly geared towards the industrial model, reinforcing its dominance.

![This artwork portrays an abstract molecular structure, symbolizing the environmental impact of pollution. Spheres transition from a cracked exterior to a green core, illustrating sustainable solutions. The network reflects systems thinking in environmental sustainability efforts to mitigate climate change, promoting responsible resource management, pollution prevention, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-molecular-structure-depicting-environmental-impact-and-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Molecular Structure Depicting Environmental Impact and Sustainable Solutions through Materials Science and Innovation")

#### Outcomes of Financial Influence on Transition

The dominance of conventional financial logic yields several critical outcomes for the agro-ecological transition. Firstly, it creates a significant capital barrier for transitioning farms and businesses. Accessing financing for diversified, small-scale, or ecological projects can be challenging, as traditional lenders lack models to assess their unique risks and revenue streams. Secondly, it can inadvertently incentivize ‘greenwashing’ or superficial adoption of certain ecological practices (e.g., precision agriculture for input efficiency) without fundamentally altering the industrial model’s structure or ecological impact.

The focus remains on optimizing the existing system rather than transforming it. Thirdly, it can exacerbate land concentration, pushing smaller, potentially more agro-ecologically oriented farms off the land as land values rise, driven by speculative investment rather than agricultural productivity based on ecological principles.

However, understanding this influence also reveals potential leverage points. Academic inquiry examines innovative financial mechanisms emerging to support agro-ecology, such as impact investing, crowdfunding for ecological farm projects, blended finance models combining public and private capital, and the development of financial instruments specifically designed to value ecosystem services or long-term soil health. Research also explores how policy can redirect financial flows, for example, through public procurement standards favoring agro-ecological products, subsidies linked to ecological outcomes rather than input use, or regulations increasing transparency in agricultural financial markets.

The specification of the challenge here involves recognizing that transitioning agricultural systems requires concurrently transitioning the financial systems that underpin them. The essence of this academic analysis lies in exposing the deep structural impediments and identifying transformative pathways within the complex interplay of ecology, society, and capital.

Financial Systems’ Influence on Agro-ecological Transition

| Mechanism of Influence | Impact on Transition | Potential Counter-Strategy (Academic/Expert Focus) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Capital** allocation favoring large-scale, uniform operations | Reinforces industrial model, limits finance for diversified/small farms | Develop and promote impact investing criteria for ecological outcomes; Blended finance models; Risk assessment models for agro-ecological diversity. |
| **Investment** focus on short-term financial returns | Disincentivizes long-term ecological investments (e.g., soil building) | Create financial instruments valuing long-term assets (soil carbon credits, biodiversity offsets); Policy incentives for long-term ecological stewardship contracts. |
| **Commodity** market standardization and price volatility | Favors monoculture; undermines diverse, local market development | Support decentralized market infrastructure; Develop mechanisms valuing product differentiation based on ecological practices; Public procurement reform. |
| **Land** speculation driven by financial asset status | Increases land costs, displaces smaller farms, incentivizes maximum extractive use | Implement land value taxes; Support community land trusts; Policy to limit foreign/institutional ownership of farmland; Tie subsidies/support to ecological land management. |

Academic inquiry into agro-ecology confronts the power dynamics embedded within global financial systems shaping food production.

Furthermore, scholarly work highlights the importance of diverse knowledge systems in countering dominant financial narratives. By validating farmer innovations, indigenous practices, and community-based monitoring of ecological health, researchers provide counter-evidence to the notion that only large-scale, technology-driven industrial models are efficient or profitable. This creates space for alternative visions of agricultural success that align with ecological and social goals.

The denotation of transition at this academic apex involves a profound deconstruction of existing power structures and a collective construction of alternatives, recognizing that the battle for a resilient food [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  is also a battle over narratives, knowledge, and capital flows. This deep dive into a single cross-sectoral influence reveals the immense complexity and the scale of the challenge, demanding expert attention and concerted, strategic action across finance, policy, research, and farming communities to genuinely steer the trajectory towards a truly agro-ecological future.

--- SOURCE 3: Agroecological Transitions → Term ---
URL: https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/term/agroecological-transitions/

SUMMARY:
Fundamentals. Agroecological Transitions represent a fundamental shift in how food is produced, distributed, and consumed.

FULL CONTENT:

# Agroecological Transitions

![This minimalist abstract design showcases an interwoven ribbon loop, representing sustainable fashion. The textured grey-silver ribbon, reminiscent of recycled materials, embodies ethical design principles and a commitment to responsible sourcing. Against a black backdrop, the artwork emphasizes form and texture, promoting conscious consumption and a circular economy approach.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-minimalist-interwoven-ribbon-loop-design-representing-sustainable.jpg "Abstract Minimalist Interwoven Ribbon Loop Design Representing Sustainable Fashion Principles on Black Background")

## Fundamentals

Agroecological Transitions represent a fundamental shift in how food is produced, distributed, and consumed. At its core, this concept moves beyond merely adopting isolated sustainable practices on a farm; it signifies a comprehensive transformation of the entire agricultural system towards ecological integrity, social equity, and economic viability. This isn’t simply about changing techniques, but about reshaping the relationships between farmers, consumers, ecosystems, and the broader society.

Understanding this transition requires recognizing the limitations and externalities of the dominant industrial agricultural model. That system, often characterized by monocultures, heavy reliance on synthetic inputs, and large-scale operations, has delivered high yields of specific commodities but at significant environmental and social costs. Soil degradation, water pollution, biodiversity loss, and vulnerability to [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  shocks are direct consequences. Furthermore, it frequently marginalizes smallholder farmers and erodes rural livelihoods.

Agroecological Transitions signify a systemic shift away from industrial agriculture towards ecologically sound and socially just food systems.

The journey towards an agroecological system involves a process of learning and adaptation. It encourages farmers to observe natural processes and mimic them in their farming practices. This might involve integrating livestock and crops, using cover crops to improve soil health, implementing complex rotations, or employing biological pest control methods. These practices are not ends in themselves, but components of a larger strategy to build resilient, self-regulating agricultural ecosystems.

![Abstract fashion design showcases sustainable raw texture through a diagonally cracked, dark grey band against a black backdrop. This image evokes ethical and conscious style, promoting responsible design and minimalist aesthetics. The rough texture highlights repurpose potential, encouraging mindful consumption and challenging fast fashion culture.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-dark-design-depicting-sustainable-raw-texture-in-fashion-and-style.jpg "Abstract Dark Design Depicting Sustainable Raw Texture in Fashion and Style with Ethical Consumption Reflection")

### Core Principles of Agroecology

Several guiding principles underpin the agroecological approach. These principles provide a framework for understanding the goals and methods involved in the transition process. They emphasize working with nature rather than attempting to dominate it.

![Industrial remnants and discarded plastic epitomize unsustainable consumption in this stark image. Concrete blocks bound with plastic strapping sit amidst refuse, symbolizing environmental pollution and the urgent need for ethical fashion practices. The desolate scene underscores the impact of consumerism, highlighting resource depletion and the non-biodegradable waste crisis.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/bleak-industrial-waste-landscape-concrete-blocks-bound-in-plastic-symbolizing.jpg "Bleak Industrial Waste Landscape Concrete Blocks Bound in Plastic Symbolizing Unsustainable Design and Consumerism")

### Why Transition?

The impetus for transition arises from multiple pressures. Environmental degradation necessitates alternative farming methods. Growing consumer demand for sustainably produced food creates market opportunities.

Policy shifts, driven by climate change concerns and food security imperatives, increasingly favor ecological approaches. Additionally, many farming communities seek greater autonomy and control over their production systems, moving away from dependence on external corporations for seeds, fertilizers, and markets.

Transitioning is not a one-size-fits-all solution; it is highly context-specific. What works in one region or for one type of farm may not be appropriate elsewhere. Success depends on understanding local ecological conditions, social dynamics, and economic realities. It requires experimentation, knowledge sharing, and collaborative action among farmers, researchers, policymakers, and consumers.

![Close up portrait highlighting natural beauty and timeless style as key components of sustainable fashion. The subject's minimalist black turtleneck represents durable clothing and conscious consumption, moving away from fast fashion. Freckles symbolize body positivity and inclusivity within ethical fashion.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/intense-gaze-portraiture-championing-sustainable-fashion-through-timeless.jpg "Intense Gaze Portraiture Championing Sustainable Fashion through Timeless Minimalist Style and Natural Beauty")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the foundational concepts, Agroecological Transitions are best understood as dynamic, multi-dimensional processes involving significant systemic change. This transition isn’t a simple switch from one set of practices to another, but rather a complex journey requiring adjustments across various scales → from the farm field to regional [food systems Meaning → Complex network of activities for producing, processing, distributing, consuming food, impacting planet & society.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/term/food-systems/)  and national policies. It involves not only technical modifications in farming but also shifts in social organization, economic models, and cultural perspectives regarding food and agriculture.

Analyzing the intermediate level of [agroecological transition Meaning → Agroecological Transition: Shifting agriculture towards ecological balance and social equity for sustainable food systems.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/term/agroecological-transition/)  necessitates examining the practical steps involved and the challenges encountered during implementation. Farmers embarking on this path often face initial yield reductions as they decrease reliance on synthetic inputs and build natural system functions. They require access to different types of knowledge, often drawing on traditional practices combined with modern ecological science. Furthermore, the economic viability can be uncertain, as markets for agroecologically produced goods may be nascent or require new value chain development.

The transition involves navigating technical, social, and economic complexities across multiple scales.

Consider the shift from a maize monoculture dependent on synthetic fertilizers and pesticides to a diversified system incorporating cover crops, livestock integration, and complex rotations. This change demands new skills in managing biological interactions, understanding nutrient cycling without synthetic inputs, and dealing with a wider variety of crops and animals. It also requires different infrastructure, potentially smaller, more flexible machinery, and new strategies for marketing diverse products. The economic model shifts from selling bulk commodities to potentially higher-value, niche products, often requiring direct sales or participation in local food networks.

![Minimalist white outdoor furniture appears lost in a stark dark landscape illustrating sustainable design challenges and environmental impact. This aerial view with its high contrast and desolate aesthetic highlights urban waste and the need for responsible sourcing in furniture and design. The scene prompts reflection on conscious consumption, lifecycle thinking, and circular design principles promoting resource efficiency and a reduced ecological footprint.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/minimalist-outdoor-furniture-amidst-urban-wasteland-depicting-sustainable.jpg "Minimalist Outdoor Furniture Amidst Urban Wasteland Depicting Sustainable Design Dilemmas and Environmental Impact")

### Stages of Transition

Researchers often describe agroecological transition as occurring in stages, though these are not always linear and can overlap. Understanding these stages helps identify the support needed at different points in the process.

![In this evocative grayscale scene, nature dramatically reclaims an abandoned office space, showcasing urban decay and material lifecycle through a minimalist lens. Overgrowth dominates desks and concrete floors, highlighting resource depletion and unsustainable design practices. This expert-like perspective on environmental impact presents a powerful visual statement on ethical considerations and conscious style choices, emphasizing the necessity for circular economy principles and a shift towards responsible, slow fashion approaches within the design and fashion industries.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/nature-recovers-urban-spaces-an-expert-view-on-sustainable-abandonment-and.jpg "Nature Recovers Urban Spaces an Expert View on Sustainable Abandonment and Minimalist Post Industrial Style")

### Challenges and Enablers

The path is fraught with challenges. Access to appropriate knowledge and technical support is crucial but often lacking. Financial constraints can impede investment in new practices or infrastructure.

Policy environments frequently favor industrial agriculture through subsidies and regulations, creating an uneven playing field. Social and cultural norms, deeply ingrained from decades of promoting industrial models, can also present resistance.

Key Challenges and Potential Enablers for Agroecological Transition

| Challenge | Potential Enabler |
| --- | --- |
| Lack of technical knowledge for ecological management | Farmer-to-farmer learning networks, participatory research |
| Financial risks during transition period | Targeted subsidies, transition finance schemes, value chain development |
| Unsupportive policy and regulatory frameworks | Policy advocacy, multi-stakeholder platforms, public procurement policies |
| Dominance of conventional markets and supply chains | Development of alternative markets, certification schemes, direct marketing support |

Conversely, several factors can enable successful transitions. Peer-to-peer learning among farmers is incredibly powerful, fostering trust and practical knowledge exchange. Supportive policies, such as those providing financial incentives for ecological practices or [investing Meaning → Investing, through an ESG lens, involves allocating capital to companies that demonstrate strong environmental, social, and governance practices.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  in public research on agroecology, are vital. Developing alternative markets that value ecological and social attributes of food provides economic incentives.

Collective action, through farmer organizations and civil society movements, can drive policy change and build resilient local food systems. Organizations like IFOAM – Organics International and regional farmer networks actively promote these enablers through capacity building and advocacy.

![Elegant sustainable design presented by a draped black textile against a dark background. The minimalist aesthetic emphasizes timeless style and eco-conscious fashion. Expert draping showcases resourceful material use and waste reduction in textile production, demonstrating ethical design.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-textile-artistry-elegant-black-drape-demonstrating-eco-conscious.jpg "Sustainable Textile Artistry Elegant Black Drape Demonstrating Eco Conscious Minimalist Design Innovation")

## Academic

From an academic standpoint, Agroecological Transitions constitute a complex, multi-scalar process of systemic transformation within agri-food systems, characterized by the co-evolution of ecological processes, social structures, economic arrangements, and political dynamics. This perspective moves beyond viewing agroecology solely as a set of farming practices, positioning it instead as a science, a set of practices, and a social movement aimed at fundamentally restructuring food production and [consumption Meaning → Consumption refers to the use of goods and services, with a sustainable focus on mindful, responsible, and circular patterns.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  to align with ecological principles and social justice imperatives. Scholarly discourse interrogates the inherent tensions and power asymmetries that shape the feasibility and trajectory of these transitions across diverse geographical and socio-political contexts.

Defining Agroecological Transitions at this level necessitates acknowledging its contested nature. It is not a monolithic concept but rather a spectrum of approaches and interpretations, ranging from those focusing primarily on on-farm ecological intensification (‘ecological modernization’ within the existing system) to those advocating for radical socio-political change to dismantle the industrial food regime (‘food sovereignty’ movements). Academic analysis delves into these different framings, examining their underlying assumptions, goals, and implications for various actors within the food system. The transition is viewed as a process of renegotiating relationships between humans and nature, and among humans themselves, regarding food.

Agroecological transitions represent a deeply political process challenging existing power structures within the global food system.

A critical academic perspective reveals that the most significant barriers to widespread agroecological adoption are often not technical or even purely economic, but deeply rooted in the political economy of food and agriculture. The prevailing structure of the global food system, dominated by a relatively small number of multinational corporations controlling inputs, processing, and retail, creates powerful inertia against systemic change. These corporations benefit from the current industrial model and actively lobby against policies that would support diversified, decentralized, and ecologically intensive systems. This corporate influence shapes research agendas, policy frameworks, and market access, effectively locking in unsustainable practices and marginalizing agroecological alternatives.

![A starkly beautiful portrait showcasing minimalist fashion choices with a dark turtleneck, embodying ethical style. The subject's contemplative gaze and simple garment suggest a commitment to sustainable practices and conscious design. This image highlights slow fashion, mindful consumption, and durable clothing, rejecting fleeting trends.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/contemplative-portrait-of-individual-embodying-minimalist-wardrobe-with.jpg "Contemplative Portrait of Individual Embodying Minimalist Wardrobe with Sustainable Turtleneck and Ethical Style Choices")

### The Political Economy of Transition

Examining the transition through the lens of political economy highlights how [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  dynamics influence the adoption and scaling of agroecology.

Consider the case of seed systems. Industrial agriculture relies heavily on a few global corporations for hybrid or genetically modified seeds, often linked to specific chemical inputs. An agroecological transition requires diverse, open-pollinated, locally adapted seeds, often managed and exchanged by farmers themselves.

This directly challenges the corporate seed model. Organizations like [Navdanya](https://www.navdanya.org/) in India exemplify efforts to reclaim seed sovereignty as a fundamental part of agroecological transition, facing direct opposition from agribusiness giants.

![A model embodies conscious style in a black and white turtleneck dress, a sustainable fashion piece representing timeless design. The minimalist garment with a black belt highlights a classic silhouette, promoting enduring design and ethical production. This image suggests mindful consumption and responsible fashion, encouraging eco-conscious choices for a versatile wardrobe.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/elegant-sustainable-fashion-statement-featuring-timeless-black-and-white.jpg "Elegant Sustainable Fashion Statement Featuring Timeless Black and White Turtleneck Dress with Minimalist Design")

### Analyzing Cross-Sectoral Influences

Agroecological transitions are profoundly influenced by dynamics in sectors seemingly outside agriculture. [Energy Meaning → The Energy domain delves into the transition from fossil fuels to renewable sources and the future of power generation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  policy dictates the cost of synthetic inputs and transportation. Water policy determines access and pricing for irrigation, impacting water-intensive industrial practices versus more water-efficient ecological ones. Trade agreements can either support local food systems and diverse production or reinforce global commodity chains favoring industrial scale.

Health policy increasingly recognizes the links between diet, agricultural practices, and public health, potentially creating demand for agroecologically produced food free from pesticide residues. Urban planning influences access to land for urban agriculture and the development of local food hubs.

Cross-Sectoral Influences on Agroecological Transitions

| Influencing Sector | Impact on Transition | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Energy | Cost of synthetic fertilizers (energy-intensive), feasibility of local processing/distribution | Rising energy prices make synthetic fertilizers more expensive, potentially favoring nutrient cycling approaches. |
| Water | Availability and cost of irrigation, impact of agricultural runoff on water quality | Policies promoting water conservation in agriculture can incentivize less water-intensive agroecological practices. |
| Trade | Competitiveness of local vs. imported food, influence on commodity prices | Trade agreements favoring large-scale commodity exports can undermine local markets for diversified agroecological products. |
| Health | Recognition of links between diet, environmental health, and public health outcomes | Public health campaigns promoting diets based on diverse, locally produced food can increase demand for agroecological products. |

Focusing on the financial sector as a key cross-sectoral influence reveals significant leverage points and barriers. Investment flows largely dictate the direction of agricultural development. Currently, vast sums are invested in agribusinesses perpetuating the industrial model. Shifting even a fraction of this capital towards supporting diversified, small-scale, ecologically sound farming systems could accelerate transitions dramatically.

This requires developing new financial products (e.g., patient capital, impact investing vehicles tailored to agroecology), changing risk assessment models in banking, and potentially using public finance to de-risk agroecological investments. The work of organizations like the Global Alliance for the [Future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  of Food highlights the need to redirect financial flows towards sustainable and equitable food systems, explicitly including agroecology. Without addressing the financial architecture that underpins the industrial food system, technical and social innovations in agroecology will struggle to scale against the prevailing economic currents. This suggests that a genuine transition necessitates a parallel transformation in how agriculture is financed, a perspective often overlooked in purely agronomic discussions of agroecology.

![A close up on a durable black ball bearing mechanism embodies sustainable design principles for fashion accessories. The use of recycled metal or sustainable composite materials highlights a commitment to ethical production and circular economy practices. This robust component suggests longevity and reduced material consumption in line with slow fashion values and eco conscious design, aiming for timeless functional style and responsible consumption.](https://fashion.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/durable-recycled-metal-ball-bearing-component-exemplifying-material-longevity.jpg "Durable Recycled Metal Ball Bearing Component Exemplifying Material Longevity in Sustainable Accessory Design")

### Long-Term Consequences and Success Insights

Successful, widespread agroecological transitions hold the potential for profound long-term consequences. Ecologically, they promise enhanced biodiversity, improved soil and water quality, reduced greenhouse gas emissions, and increased resilience to climate change impacts. Socially, they can revitalize rural communities, improve farmer livelihoods, enhance food security and nutrition through diversified diets, and strengthen local economies. Economically, while potentially shifting profit distribution, they can create new value chains, reduce dependence on volatile input markets, and build more stable, localized economies.

Insights from successful transitions globally underscore the importance of collective action and supportive institutions. Farmer cooperatives and associations play a vital role in knowledge sharing, collective marketing, and political advocacy. Public institutions, when aligned, can provide essential research, extension services, and policy support. Civil society organizations are crucial in raising awareness, building alternative food networks, and challenging unfavorable policies.

Ultimately, achieving a widespread agroecological transition requires a fundamental power shift, empowering farmers and communities to regain control over their food systems from dominant corporate interests. This perspective, while challenging to conventional economic models, is increasingly supported by evidence showing the environmental and social benefits of diverse, localized, and ecologically managed food production.

URL: https://collaboratif.cirad.fr/alfresco/s/d/workspace/SpacesStore/786f5e44-55a5-4083-a0b8-c14f3c9ebb20/Plaq%2520AE%2520AFD%2520ENG.pdf

SUMMARY:
For most family farming systems, the transition means a direct switch to systems with high environmental and social value, without first trying intensive

FULL CONTENT:
SUPPORTING THE AGRO-ECOLOGICAL TRANSITION IN THE GLOBAL SOUTH FEEDBACK ON THE EXPERIENCES OF CIRAD AND AFD Farming systems in the global South face specific challenges relating to population growth, rapid urbanization, climate disruption, State disengagement, the power of globalized agro-industry, and a lack of investment and services.
As a result, they need to innovate in order to develop adaptation and resilience solutions. CIRAD and AFD, together with researchers and agricultural development stakeholders in the global South, are working on research and full-scale trials aimed at documenting the capacity of agro-ecology to provide an economically and socially viable alternative to conventional intensification models.
For CIRAD and AFD, agro-ecology is:   a scientific discipline that combines the concepts of scientific ecology and agronomy,   a type of engineering based on research results and producer know-how,   a contribution to developing sustainable food systems, by boosting various services (economic, social and environmental) in addition to the production function. The term “ecological intensification” is used to stress the need to reconcile increased agricultural production with the principles of agro-ecology. The agro-ecological transition refers to the range of interconnected dynamic processes by which new, sustainable cropping systems based on the principles of agro-ecology are gradually replacing conventional systems.
Based on Michel Griffon’s representation (2013), CIRAD and AFD have classed the main levers of the agro-ecological transition along two main axes:   the vertical axis is biophysical. It relates to the grow-ing use of functional biodiversity in agro-ecological systems instead of chemical inputs (mainly pesticides) and fossil-based products. This axis also takes account of the target of improving resource use efficiency (water, energy, soils, etc) and of optimizing bio- geochemical cycles within production systems.   the horizontal axis relates to organization and insti-tutions. It reflects the determining role of concerted dynamics involving producers and the whole range of agricultural development players in building agro-ecological systems, from a plot to a territory scale. The agro-ecological transition is thus the capacity to develop innova-tive production systems by making use of biological, organizational and institutional levers.
THE LEVERS OF THE AGRO-ECOLOGICAL TRANSITION 1 Adapted from M. Griffon, in: “Qu’est-ce que l’agriculture écologiquement intensive ?” M. Griffon. Editions Quae, 2013 > 2 BIOLOGICAL LEVERS OF THE AGRO-ECOLOGICAL TRANSITION   Making use of biodiversity within agrosystems (intra- and interspecific mixes, service plants, nitrogen-fixing plants, rotations, etc) to foster natural control of crop pests and boost the range of regulatory services, hence reducing use of chemical inputs, particularly pesticides.
  Maximizing biomass production by intercepting sunlight and fostering carbon capture in plants and in the soil (permanent soil cover).
  Fostering crop-livestock farming interactions.
  Recycling resources and optimizing water and nutrient bio-geochemical cycles to ensure more efficient use and cut losses and pollution (for instance through organic matter management).
  Revising genetic improvement targets to take better account of the interactions between the various species and their environment.
  Managing landscapes by organizing landscape mosaics to supplement regulation. ORGANIZATIONAL AND INSTITUTIONAL LEVERS OF THE AGRO-ECOLOGICAL TRANSITION   Developing producer support services in terms of training, advice and technical support, and coor-dinating them with other services, with research and with other agrifood supply chain operators.
  Boosting the involvement of upstream and down-stream supply chain operators (supplies of inputs and suitable mechanized equipment, of seeds, etc), studying consumer requirements and expectations within a short- and long-term forward-looking vision, fostering product marketing, and develop-ing standards for recognizing goods produced in systems with high environmental and social value.
  Developing capitalization, teaching, knowledge transmission (scientific or drawn from producer practices), use of feedback on experiences, pro-ducer training (peer-to-peer, demonstrations, trials).
  Fostering political and institutional commitment to guarantee support for local and territorial initi-atives through appropriate policy and legal frame-works (laws, regulations, and economic, financial and tax instruments).
  Respecting the range of producer strategies, including on a local level, and encouraging the active participation and voluntary involvement of producers in changing their systems; in this respect, the existence of forms of local collective action and management methods for material commons (nurseries, seeds and animals, water, land, pasture and forest cover resources, etc) and immaterial commons (knowledge, information, social capital social, etc) is a powerful lever.
NATURAL ECOSYSTEMS LOW-INPUT TRADITIONAL SYSTEMS ECOLOGICALLY INTENSIVE SYSTEMS Mobilization of biodiversity Aggradation Mobilization of stakeholders and organizations, organized dynamics Natural dynamics Uniformization Degradation CONVENTIONAL INTENSIVE SYSTEMS The agro-ecological transition concerns the various types of farming systems in the global South In the countries of the global South, ensuring that family farming adapts to the challenges posed by global change is a priority. For most family farming systems, the transition means a direct switch to systems with high environmental and social value, without first trying intensive conventional systems based on massive use of chemical inputs. However, agro-ecological transitions must also apply to production systems based on intensive monocultures with high chem-ical input use, since the environmental and social sustain-ability of such systems is under particular scrutiny.
CIRAD and AFD are supporting those involved in the agro-ecological transition in the global South The work being done by CIRAD and AFD fits into an overall participatory, territory-based research-action approach aimed at co-building several commons: knowledge (scien-tific and endogenous); partnerships (involving producer organizations, groups, networks, innovation platforms, operators, NGOs, which are playing a vital role in the agro-ecological transition and with which CIRAD and AFD work, notably the GTAE [working group on the agro-ecological transition]; capacity (training, increas-ing social capital, knowledge development); and lastly innovation processes themselves (with a capacity to go beyond the pilot approach). CIRAD and AFD have conducted several dozen development projects centring on the agro-ecological transition, primarily in sub-Saharan Africa, Madagascar, Southeast Asia, Central America, Latin America, the West Indies and the Indian Ocean. 3 4 ▲ COVER PLANT, CROTALARIA SPECTABILIS, IN A BANANA PLANTING © Hoa Tran Quoc/CIRAD More than ten years spent conducting experiments and supporting development projects have enabled CIRAD and AFD to pinpoint the generic conditions for successful agro-ecological transitions:   inclusion, which puts producers at the heart of the debate and innovation process and which must focus more on the specific role of women;   contextualization, since the agro-ecological transition cannot be seen as universal or homogeneous, or expected to happen overnight; on the contrary, it takes many shapes and forms, and is site-specific (local, territorial or regional), sometimes sector-specific (supply chain) and dependent on the local context and local constraints and opportunities;   scaling, which means organizing gradual stages of co-design of new systems;   opening up, encouraging producers to interact with other local players so as to identify the measures required to support the transition; this process is a fundamental part of skill buil-ding;   institutional structuring, within formal partnership structures (innovation platforms and networks, technical advice, other networks, etc) and specific tools (demonstrations, expe-riments, role play, adaptive modelling, etc) to foster exchanges;   public action, which is vital for supporting transitions (political, legal, regulatory and finan-cial frameworks, economic instruments;   capitalization and transverse analyses of experiences, dissemination of results and expe-rience sharing, to ensure that contextualization is not an obstacle to transition, and that the principles and general rules of transition are combined with specific instruments for local implementation of solutions.
FEEDBACK ON EXPERIENCES 2   Switching from intensive conventional monocultures to multi-species systems to reduce environmental impact The chlordecone crisis, the subsequent social and political pressure and a move on the part of the supply chain and producers resulted in the drafting of a “Plan Banane Durable” (Sustainable Banana Plan). Producers and the research sector already had a varied technical package resulting from pre-vious innovations (in vitro plantlets, biological control of weevils, etc), and had developed multi-species systems and service plant engineering operations. These solutions have very significantly reduced pesti-cide use. Environmental performance indicators have been developed. A dedicated innovation platform and technical institute have been set up. The plan is led by the supply chain and producers, CIRAD, the Guadeloupe and Martinique Regional Councils, the State and the EU.
THE CASE OF WEST INDIAN BANANAS 5 ▲ UPLAND RICE LANDSCAPE IN MADAGASCAR © J. Dusserre/CIRAD   Developing agro-ecological systems to satisfy food demand   Adapting to climate change and market evolutions UPLAND RICE IN THE HIGHLANDS OF MADAGASCAR In the central plateau region of Madagascar, popu-lation growth and increased demand for rice led farmers to grow rice under upland conditions, to supplement irrigated lowland produc-tion. They ran up against problems of fertility and pests (weeds, fungal diseases, soil insects), with limited access to the means of conventional intensification. CIRAD and its partners, with the help of AFD, have worked with them to find agro-ecological intensifi-cation solutions, making use of three technical levers: breeding rice varieties suited to upland conditions, improving soils, and practising conservation agricul-ture using service plants. A wide range of possible solutions is on offer, which is enabling many of the country’s poorest farmers to produce food crops in a sustainable way.
COFFEE-BASED AGROFORESTRY SYSTEMS IN CENTRAL AMERICA The global coffee price crisis of the 2000s trigge-red deep social crises in Central America. Producers turned to more protected markets that were also more restrictive in terms of environmental protection. CIRAD and its partners have looked into two comple-mentary solutions: on the one hand, they have studied the links between different agroforestry practices (species selection and management) and associated ecosystem services in coffee plantings, and on the other, they have created new coffee varieties. Tech-nical options based on competition between species have been tested within producer networks. Local innovation platforms and payments for environmental services have been introduced. National laws to promote agro-ecology have been adopted and private firms have developed environmental labels and quality clusters.
▲ COFFEE PLANTING UNDER TIMBER TREES (TABEBUIA ROSEA) © B. Rapidel/CIRAD   Responding to increased local demand and land use pressure In the cotton-growing savannah zones of western Burkina Faso, researchers, development players and producers have worked together to help poly-culture-livestock systems make the agro-ecological transition, by encouraging the association of crop and animal farming on a plot, herd, farm, territory and supply chain scale.
Innovative techniques for organic fertilizer manage-ment (field pits), growing fodder crops alone or inter-cropped with cereals (velvet bean, cowpea, fodder trees) and more intensive, integrated mixed sys-tems (dairy and beef fattening workshops), and local ▲ FERTILIZER PIT, BURKINA FASO © M. Laurent/CIRAD FIELD TRIALS, LAOS © P. Lienhard/CIRAD t territorial governance structures have been developed. Recent studies have assessed their initial impact: increased production, better closure of nutrient cycles within agro-ecosystems, inclusion of women and young people in local value chains, etc.
 Combining local and regional approaches SUPPORTING THE AGRO-ECOLOGICAL TRANSITION IN LAOS The hills of northern Laos are rapidly opening up to agriculture. Maize and cassava, which have a parti-cularly strong impact on the environment and health, are developing. This means mechanized, highly ero-sive soil tillage, anarchic use of herbicides and high rates of chemical fertilizers. CIRAD, AFD and their partners have launched two projects to support the agro-ecological transition in these regions, where agrarian dynamics are largely determined by private supply chain operators. The first set out to foster participatory drafting of land use plans on an agri-cultural community scale, incorporating innovative agro-ecological practices; it involves capacity building among extension staff and suggests a novel method to monitor and assess the resilience of agricultural communities as regards external events (economic, climate-related, etc). The second, which is more wide-ranging, aims to generate and share knowledge in the field of agro-ecology, and to boost the credi-bility of the agro-ecological movement as regards policy-makers and consumers in the region.
6 POLYCULTURE-LIVESTOCK SYSTEMS IN WESTERN BURKINA FASO Three key questions must continue to be documented:   How, from a technical and organizational point of view, is it possible to switch gradually from conventional agriculture to agriculture based on the principles of agro-ecology?
  What is the cost/extra cost of the transition and what mechanisms are required to allow for that cost?
  What specific types of support are required in the global South as regards the agro-ecological transition? A range of operations must be continued to this end:   Boosting knowledge of the ecological mechanisms of regulation   Building methods and capacity for multi-criteria and multi-scale assessments of system performance in order to characterize the relevance and the economic, social and environmental impact of potential solutions and assess the compro-mises required   Paying greater attention to labour productivity and to improving working condi-tions, since the agro-ecological transition is often labour-intensive and based on practices that involve taking extra care of crops THE CHALLENGES TO BE TACKLED   Promoting “healthy” vegetable production A PRODUCTION LABEL FOR VIETNAM Reducing pesticide use satisfies consumers’ demands but increases produ-cers’ costs. To allow them to benefit from higher prices, they are being offered outlets and specific labelling as part of research and development pro-jects. “Healthy” vegetable producer groups around Hanoi are one example. ▲“HEALTHY” VEGETABLES ON SALE AT A MARKET, VIETNAM © P. Moustier/CIRAD 3 7 […] Editing: Christine Nouaille Translation: Helen Burford-Buttazzoni, CIRAD Cover photo: Jatiluwih rice terraces, Bali, Indonesia A. Rival © CIRAD Illustrations: Delphine Guard, CIRAD.
Graphics: Patricia Doucet, CIRAD. Laurence Laffont © AFD-CIRAD,February 2018.
[…]   Developing participatory ways of building crop management sequences and production systems   Recognizing the vital importance of territories in the agro-ecological transition, and parti-cularly looking into the potential compromises and synergies between the different produc-tion models and practices that co-exist within a territory, so as to draft policies that support their co-existence and transformation pathways   Assessing the merits of agro-ecology as a way of adapting to climate change and contribu-ting to mitigating greenhouse gas emissions   Capitalizing on the knowledge gained from the various experiences of agro-ecological tran-sition in various contexts in the global South   Building an overall approach encompassing the challenges of agricultural production, public health and territorial development, with a view to establishing new food systems. 5 rue Roland Barthes 75598 Paris Cedex 12 France Tel. +33 1 53 44 31 31 www.afd.fr Contact Emmanuelle Poirier-Magona poirier-magonae@afd.fr 42 rue Scheffer 75116 Paris France Tel. +33 1 53 70 20 00 www.cirad.fr Contact François Cote francois.cote@cirad.fr 8

--- SOURCE 5: What is agroecology? | Soil Association ---
URL: https://www.soilassociation.org/causes-campaigns/a-ten-year-transition-to-agroecology/what-is-agroecology/

SUMMARY:
# What is agroecology? ## Agroecology is sustainable farming that works with nature Agroecology is about how we design and manage sustainable food and farming systems. The Ten Years for Agroecology project models how the UK and the rest of Europe can transition to a food and farming model to reduce climate impacts, support wildlife to bounce back and feed a growing population a healthy diet. Organic farming is a form of agroecological farming. The easiest way you can support agroecological farming is to engage with your local farmers and growers. However, to achieve an agroecological future, everybody – farmers, caterers, shops and citizens – needs to support a new way of thinking about food, farming and the environment.

FULL CONTENT:
![Soil Association](/images/charity/logo.png)
![Soil.jpg (1)](/media/14537/soil.jpg?center=0.90909090909090906,0.46341463414634149&mode=crop&width=1903&height=479&rnd=131643061380000000)

# What is agroecology?

## Agroecology is sustainable farming that works with nature

Agroecology is about how we design and manage sustainable food and farming systems. It's an approach that applies both ecological (the relationship between plants, animals, humans and the environment) and social principles.

Agroecological systems are designed from the ground up, with people at the centre. They respond to local contexts, challenges and opportunities. [There are 10 common principles to agroecology as outlined by the Food and Agriculture Organisation of the United Nations](https://www.fao.org/agroecology/overview/en/).

#### Agroecology in action

[Agroforestry](/causes-campaigns/agroforestry/what-is-agroforestry/ "What Is Agroforestry?") is a great example of agroecology. It's the practice of combining trees and farming. It demonstrates how food production and nature can co-exist.

Grazing farm animals under trees gives them shelter and fodder, whilst their manure enriches the soil. And planting trees on land normally used to grow cereal crops can provide another crop - be that fruit, nuts or timber. This provides another income stream for farmers and also protects soils from erosion, as the trees' deep roots help create a healthy soil structure.

Agroforestry, like many agroecological approaches, is a win-win.

![Chickens and agroforestry](/media/12142/david-brass-credit-phil-formby.jpg)

![Chickens and agroforestry](/media/12142/david-brass-credit-phil-formby.jpg)

#### **Agroecology: a truly sustainable alternative**

At global and local levels, we face multiple food system challenges - flooding, soil degradation, biodiversity collapse, malnutrition and obesity.

Intensive farming systems contribute to these problems. They exhaust natural resources focusing on short-term gains rather than the long-term sustainability that works best for the land, wildlife and local communities. We need an alternative food system that is truly sustainable. The good news is, many of the solutions lie in agroecology.

The [Ten Years for Agroecology project](/causes-campaigns/a-ten-year-transition-to-agroecology/iddri-report-ten-years-for-agroecology-in-europe/ "IDDRI report: Ten Years for Agroecology in Europe") models how the UK and the rest of Europe can transition to a food and farming model to reduce climate impacts, support wildlife to bounce back and feed a growing population a healthy diet.

![Potatoes being dug in field](/media/5837/potatoes-being-dug-in-field.jpg)

![Potatoes being dug in field](/media/5837/potatoes-being-dug-in-field.jpg)

#### **Where does organic fit into agroecology?**

Agroecology is an umbrella term that covers lots of agricultural practices that you may be more familiar with, like organic, biodynamic or permaculture.

[Organic farming](/take-action/organic-living/what-is-organic/ "What Is Organic?") is a form of agroecological farming.

All organic farmers are required to meet a strict set of standards.

These standards guarantee higher animal welfare, fewer pesticides and antibiotics and no GMOs.

Organic practices also support more jobs on farms, healthy soil and more on-farm.

Certified organic farms are inspected on an annual basis to legally verify farmers meet these high standards. So organic is a great way to connect citizens and farmers who are using agroecological methods and support the growth of agroecology.

#### How can I support agroecology?

The easiest way you can support [agroecological farming](/causes-campaigns/a-ten-year-transition-to-agroecology/) is to engage with your local farmers and growers. Learn about their farming practices, join a local veg box scheme or [buy organic.](/take-action/organic-living/buy-organic/ "Buy Organic")

Organic food is clearly labelled and provides a guarantee that your products have been produced to some of the highest environmental standards.

However, to achieve an agroecological future, everybody – farmers, caterers, shops and citizens – needs to support a new way of thinking about food, farming and the environment.

With the support of the organic community, we continuously make the case for agroecology in Government, so policymakers understand the opportunities it offers for climate, nature and our health. We’re lobbying for more support, empowerment and advice for farmers, so they are able to explore how they can transition to agroecology.

## Want to find out more?

Sign up for updates with news, offers and practical tips.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Transition models represent systematic changes in agricultural practices and philosophies, moving from conventional methods to agroecological frameworks that prioritize sustainability and resilience.
Essential components include ecological principles, social equity, knowledge co-creation, and participatory approaches that engage various stakeholders in reshaping food systems.
Focuses on enhancing biodiversity, resource efficiency, and ecosystem health, fostering agricultural practices that work harmoniously with natural systems rather than dominating them.
Emphasizes fair access to resources and opportunities for all stakeholders, particularly marginalized groups, ensuring that agroecological practices contribute to community well-being and justice.
Aims at creating resilient economic systems that support sustainable farming practices, reduce dependency on external inputs, and foster local markets for agroecologically produced goods.
